summary: Jeff Atwood argues that software projects cannot be estimated or managed like traditional engineering projects because software development is fundamentally different in nature. Unlike construction or manufacturing where you can study hundreds of similar examples, software that has been built before can simply be purchased rather than rebuilt. This economic reality means the only software projects that get funded are novel, unproven ones that haven't been done before. Atwood concludes that software development is more akin to invention at Edison's laboratory than assembly-line manufacturing, requiring different management approaches.
key_points: Software engineering differs from other engineering because if something has been built before, you buy it rather than rebuild it
The economic reality that easy, low-risk projects don't get funded is what makes software development inherently hard and risky
Software development is invention and evolution, not assembly-line manufacturing
Traditional project management tools like MS Project are misapplied to software because managers see the word 'project' and assume similarity to construction
In software, developers define and control an entire universe rather than working within fixed physical constraints like gravity
money_quotes: The only kind of software we ever build is unproven, experimental software.
The only new software development projects undertaken are those that haven't been done before or those whose predecessors are not publicly available.
No sane business is going to spend money on building software that it can buy more economically.
In software development, if you're repeating the same project over and over, you won't have a job for very long.
We're paid to build high-risk, experimental airplanes.
Software invention has more in common with Mr. Edison's Menlo Park laboratory than the local hi-rise construction site.
In software engineering projects, you aren't subject to God's rules. You get to play God: define, build, and control an entire universe in a box.
key_insight: Software projects are inherently harder to estimate than construction projects because the economics of the industry ensure that only novel, never-before-built systems get funded—everything else gets purchased off the shelf.
